Historical Ammunition Types and PEN Modi.Description: The standard ammunition used in early muzzleloaders, made of lead and designed for smoothbore muskets.
Paper-wrapped black powder cartridges used in muzzleloaders, generally with lower velocity.
A lead ball used in early smoothbore muskets, relatively low velocity and penetration.
A conical bullet designed for rifled muskets, offering higher velocity and better penetration than round musket balls.
Early lead bullets used in revolvers, typically low-velocity with minimal penetration but useful for close combat.
The iconic Colt .45 caliber round, a standard sidearm during the American Civil War, made of lead and designed for stopping power.
Early shotgun shells filled with shot or buckshot, used black powder as propellant, offering less penetration compared to modern ammo.
Shotgun shells loaded with multiple large pellets, designed for close-range combat but reduced penetration.
Shotgun shells with smaller pellets, designed for hunting birds, with very low penetration.
Early rounds designed to penetrate armor, often made of hardened lead or early steel cores, used in the late 19th century.
Early explosive rounds used in cannons and artillery, designed to explode on impact but with reduced penetration.
Early flintlock pistol ammunition made of round lead balls, used for personal defense or duels.
Early rifled ammunition with a conical design, leading to greater accuracy and penetration in long-range shooting.
Round solid shot used in early cannons, effective for crushing or destroying structures, ships, and troops.
Artillery rounds containing small balls or pellets, used for close-range suppression fire, causing wide damage but low penetration.
Solid slug designed to increase the range and penetration power of shotgun shells, especially in early firearms.
Rounds used in early repeating rifles, such as the Henry rifle, capable of multiple shots without reloading.
Military standard for general-purpose use, rounds with full metal jackets that prevent deformation but limit expansion.
Rounds designed for higher velocity, offering improved long-range performance and penetration compared to standard ammunition.
